title: Woven market bag on a rigid heddle loom
subtitle: A plain-weave cotton bag with a gusset and twisted cord handles, off the rigid heddle
excerpt: Weave a sturdy cotton market bag on a rigid heddle loom in plain weave, then sew the woven panel into a bag with a flat bottom gusset and twisted cord handles. The finished bag holds about 5 kg comfortably and is washable.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional plain-weave construction and modern British rigid-heddle teaching. The bag construction from a flat-woven panel is documented in British guild workshop patterns.

glossaryTerms.definition: The simplest interlacement: each weft thread passes over one warp thread and under the next, alternating on each row. Produces a stable, even fabric suited to bag construction.
The number of warp threads per centimetre or per inch. A firm cotton bag is woven at a slightly closer sett than a scarf, to produce a dense, shape-holding fabric.
A triangular or rectangular insert at the base or sides of a bag that adds three-dimensional depth. For a flat-woven market bag, the gusset is formed by sewing triangles at each base corner.

content.text: market bag uses a tight cotton
on the rigid heddle and a simple sewn
at the base to create depth. The weaving part is a long beginner-friendly rectangle; the construction is basic sewing and takes about 20 minutes once the panel is off the loom.

items.name: Cotton 8/2 or 10/2 yarn for warp and weft
Rigid heddle loom, minimum 30 cm weaving width
Sewing needle and thread matching the warp colour

content.text: Warp the rigid heddle loom at 20 epi (8 ends per cm) in a 8/2 cotton for a firm bag fabric. Warp to a total woven length of 100 cm: this will produce a bag front and back each about 45 cm tall once folded and allowing for 5 cm take-up and shrinkage. Use a single colour warp, or alternate two colours for a simple stripe.

Weave 100 cm of plain weave in a matching or contrasting weft. Beat firmly on each pass so the fabric is dense and stable. Check the web width every 10 rows and adjust your weft angle (inserting the weft in a slight arc before beating) to prevent the selvedges drawing in.

Cut the panel off the loom and secure the warp ends by folding the top 3 cm to the wrong side twice and stitching a firm hem. The hem becomes the bag's top opening edge. The bottom of the panel will be sewn closed to form the base.
Step 4: Sew the bag and gusset'

Fold the panel in half with right sides together. Sew up both side seams with a firm backstitch. To form the base gusset, pinch each bottom corner so the side seam aligns with the base fold, then sew a straight line across each corner 4 cm from the point. Cut off the corner triangle, leaving a 1 cm seam allowance. Turn right side out: the base now has depth.

Cut four lengths of the warp yarn, each 2 metres long. Hold two lengths together and twist tightly in one direction, then fold the twisted length in half and allow it to ply back on itself into a firm cord. Make two cords this way. Sew each cord handle inside the bag at the top, positioning the handles about 8 cm in from each side seam.

items.problem: The bag fabric stretches under the weight of shopping
items.cause: The weft was beaten too loosely, leaving an open weave that stretches under weight.
items.fix: Beat more firmly on each pass. For a bag, the fabric should be dense enough that individual warp threads are not visible when you hold it up to the light.

items.problem: The handles tore off at the attachment point
items.cause: The stitching was only through the woven fabric at the top hem rather than secured inside the bag
items.fix: Reinforce each handle attachment with a rectangle of backstitching through both the bag fabric and the inside of the hem at each end of the handle.
